title: Review: wolftpm crates
notes:
Maintainer risk: LOW — one API contract violation in init_swtpm (returns libc error code, not wolfTPM code), one divergence risk in error display. No security surface issues, no unsafe soundness problems, no API lock-in issues beyond existing transient-key lifecycle decisions.
Confidence: 15% chance of missed bug. Least confident flags: FMT1 stripping correctness under wolfTPM-modified error codes (tested logic against spec, no live TPM). Uncertainty driven by: can't run integration tests (no /dev/tpm0); can't verify wolfTPM actually returns modifier-qualified TPM_RC_SIGNATURE in practice. Would raise confidence if: ran wolfTPM 4.0.0 against swtpm and observed actual VerifySignature failure codes.
Idiom rating: CLEAN — unsafe blocks all have SAFETY comments; ownership and lifetime design is idiomatic; error types are properly non_exhaustive; no gratuitous clones. Two issues: init_swtpm should return a typed error instead of bare i32, and hash_sz try_from is dead code in sign/verify.
Rust idiom confidence: 10% chance of missed issue. Least confident flags: none — I'm satisfied the unsafe blocks, ownership model, and error handling are correct. Uncertainty driven by: can't verify at runtime that FMT1-qualified TPM_RC_SIGNATURE is the actual return from wolfTPM2_VerifyHashScheme (no TPM hardware). Would raise confidence if: ran integration tests against swtpm.
